title: Anesthesia for Labor and Delivery
authors: Hanson R, Hoyt MCR / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Anesthesia for Labor and Delivery?
บทนี้อาจดูเหมือนเรื่อง obstetric team — แต่จริงๆ แล้วเป็น neonatal pharmacology ที่เริ่มก่อนทารกคลอด. (1) ยาทุกชนิดที่แม่ได้รับระหว่างคลอดมีโอกาสผ่าน placenta — degree ขึ้นกับ pKa, lipophilicity, protein binding, UV:MV ratio; (2) Meperidine → normeperidine: half-life 15–40 h → respiratory depression/neurologic effect ในทารกนานได้หลายวัน; (3) Epidural fever (non-infectious) → neonatologist ถูก call ทำ sepsis workup ทั้งที่ไม่มี infection — ต้องรู้จักแยก; (4) General anesthesia สำหรับ CS: ถ้า induction-to-delivery >15 min → volatile agent equilibrate → นวล depress; (5) Epidural maternal hypotension → ↓ uteroplacental perfusion → fetal acidosis ถ้าไม่ treat เร็ว. เราคือผู้ที่รับผลของ obstetric anesthesia decision — ต้องรู้เพื่อรับมือได้ถูกต้อง.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย neuroanatomy ของ labor pain: visceral (Stage 1, T10–L2) vs somatic (Stage 2, pudendal nerve) และ implication ต่อ opioid effectiveness
ระบุ pharmacokinetic principles ที่กำหนด placental transfer: ionization (pKa), lipophilicity, protein binding, UV:MV ratio, fetal ion trapping
อธิบาย nonpharmacologic methods: Lamaze/Bradley, hypnobirthing, water birth, acupuncture — evidence level, fetal risks ที่อาจมี
เปรียบเทียบ opioids ที่ใช้ใน labor: morphine, meperidine (normeperidine!), fentanyl, remifentanil, nalbuphine, buprenorphine — mechanism, half-life, neonatal effects
อธิบาย benzodiazepines ใน labor: diazepam (UV:MV →2, active metabolites 8 days) vs midazolam (low UV:MV) — neonatal effects + eclampsia context
อธิบาย nitrous oxide: mechanism (NMDA antagonism), analgesic limitations, safety data, US utilization trend
เปรียบเทียบ neuraxial techniques: lumbar epidural, spinal, CSE, PCEA, PIEB — onset, duration, drug levels, fetal exposure
ระบุ maternal side effects ของ neuraxial blocks: hypotension (mechanism + treatment), pruritus, motor block, fever (non-infectious IL-6 mechanism)
วิเคราะห์ fetal/neonatal effects ของ labor analgesia: acid-base balance, FHR changes (bradycardia 10–12%), breastfeeding — evidence review
อธิบาย general anesthesia สำหรับ CS: induction agents (propofol/ketamine/etomidate), succinylcholine (t½ 90 sec), volatile agents, failed intubation risk (1:808)
ระบุ tranexamic acid (TXA) ใน postpartum hemorrhage: WOMAN trial, neonatal exposure data, FDA category B
1. Labor Pain — Classification and Neuroanatomy
1.1 Types of Labor Pain
Visceral pain: cramping, dull, steady — จาก distension ของ lower uterine segment + cervical dilation ใน Stage 1 (dilation phase); nerves: T10–L2; opioids effective ใน visceral pain

Somatic pain: sharp, intermittent, well-localized — จาก distension ของ vagina, pelvic floor, perineum ใน Stage 2 (descent phase); nerves: pudendal nerve (S2–S4); opioids less effective ใน somatic pain
Clinical implication: systemic opioids adequate ใน Stage 1 (visceral) แต่ inadequate ใน Stage 2 (somatic) — ทำให้ IV opioid approach มีข้อจำกัด
Pain intensity: labor pain has been shown to be more intense than almost any other known pain syndrome (Melzack et al Can Med Assoc J 1981)
1.2 Why Neuraxial is Superior
Local anesthetics block nerve conduction directly — effective at both visceral + somatic pain because accessible nerve pathways are blocked en route
Modern epidurals: dilute local anesthetic + low-dose opioid → analgesia with minimal motor block + minimal fetal drug transfer
Every study comparing neuraxial vs any other analgesic = superior result for neuraxial (Anim-Somuah Cochrane 2011)
2. Nonpharmacologic Approaches
2.1 Natural Childbirth (Lamaze / Bradley)
Lamaze (1950s): controlled breathing + focal points; Bradley (1970s): adds partner as active coach. Both aim to ↓ pain perception without drugs

Fetal risk from incorrect Lamaze performance: maternal hyperventilation → ↓ PaCO2 → maternal respiratory alkalosis → oxyhemoglobin curve shifts LEFT → ↓ O2 release to fetus → fetal hypoxemia + metabolic acidosis; also ↓ umbilical blood flow
Fetal tracing: hyperventilation-induced FHR changes resemble cord compression pattern
Effective neuraxial technique PREVENTS maternal hyperventilation and ↑ fetal acid-base status (Reynolds Br J Obstet Gynecol 2002)
Studies: natural childbirth preparation did NOT ↓ epidural use; mothers who planned unmedicated birth but converted to epidural: longer labors, higher pain scores, caught unprepared
Eisenach et al Pain 2008: severity of acute pain after childbirth (not mode of delivery) predicts postpartum depression
2.2 Hypnosis, Water Birth, Acupuncture

Hypnobirthing: requires weeks of practice; Cochrane 2016 (9 trials, 2954 participants): may ↓ overall analgesic use but NOT epidural use; no clear difference in spontaneous vaginal birth or satisfaction
Water birth: Cochrane: immersion in Stage 1 ↓ neuraxial requests; Stage 2 immersion unclear; no clear evidence for or against; infection/neonatal safety questions unanswered; no substantive studies
Acupuncture: mostly used for low back pain during labor; small controlled trials show benefit but time-consuming + inconsistent; no negative fetal effect reported

Important for Neonatologist — Nonpharmacologic ≠ Zero Fetal Risk
Incorrect hyperventilation during Lamaze can produce FHR pattern resembling cord compression (↓ maternal PaCO2 → ↓ O2 to fetus → fetal acidosis). Epidural actually IMPROVES fetal acid-base status vs no analgesia or systemic opioids (Reynolds meta-analysis 2002). การสันนิษฐานว่า 'natural birth = safest for baby' ไม่ถูกต้องเสมอไป — ขึ้นกับ technique ที่แม่ใช้.
3. Pharmacokinetics and Placental Transfer
3.1 Three Key Properties Governing Placental Transfer

(1) Ionization (pKa): ionized molecules CANNOT cross membranes. Local anesthetics = weak bases (pKa 7.6–9.1). At maternal pH 7.4, more drug is ionized → stays in maternal circulation. Fetal ion trapping: if fetal pH < maternal pH (acidosis) → drug converts to ionized form in fetus → cannot exit → accumulates. Worse in compromised fetus.

(2) Lipophilicity: lipophilic drugs cross lipid membranes more readily. Most labor pain medications = lipophilic. Sufentanil > fentanyl in lipophilicity → epidural sufentanil more readily enters maternal circulation than fentanyl
(3) Protein binding: only unbound, nonionized, lipophilic drug crosses membranes. High protein binding = bulk molecule → stays in maternal circulation
Metabolism: succinylcholine t½ ≈ 90 sec (pseudocholinesterase) → no fetal exposure. Meperidine → normeperidine (active metabolite, t½ 15–40 h) → prolonged neonatal effects
3.2 Route of Administration and Maternal Blood Levels

Maternal Blood Levels by Route — Greatest to Least Fetal Exposure
IV > Paracervical > Intramuscular > Epidural > Spinal. Spinal drug levels in maternal blood: clinically irrelevant — virtually no fetal exposure from spinal technique. Epidural: low but detectable levels. Paracervical block: surprisingly high levels (injected near uterine vasculature). Two protective barriers: (1) fetal liver first-pass (40–60% of fetal-circulating drug metabolized); (2) dilutional effect in fetal general circulation.
3.3 UV:MV Ratio — Umbilical Vein:Maternal Vein Drug Ratio
4. Sedatives in Labor
4.1 Benzodiazepines

Diazepam ใน Labor — สำคัญสำหรับ Neonatologist
Diazepam: UV:MV ratio → 1 within minutes, → 2 within hours. Active metabolites half-life up to 8 days. ใช้ใน eclamptic seizures → neonatal: hypotonia, hypothermia, respiratory depression. Midazolam: low UV:MV → ไม่ถึง fetus มาก → ใช้ใน maternal anxiety ก่อน CS; anterograde maternal amnesia → มักให้หลัง delivery เพื่อ preserve birth memory. ถ้าทารก expose to diazepam: monitor hypotonia, respiratory depression, thermal instability นาน 2–8 วัน.
Oral cleft concern with 1st-trimester diazepam: studies do NOT support this association (Rosenberg NEJM 1983)
Barbiturates: phenobarbital rarely used; thiopental no longer available in US → propofol = preferred induction agent for CS
5. Opioids in Labor
5.1 Opioid Receptor Types
μ-receptor (mu): most complete analgesia; side effects: pruritus, N/V, euphoria, respiratory depression to apnea — dose-dependent, no ceiling
κ-receptor (kappa): less intense analgesia; ceiling effect — dose-response curve flattens; ceiling ALSO applies to respiratory depression → agonist-antagonists safer re: apnea
δ-receptor (delta): mediates endogenous endorphins especially at spinal cord
All opioids: more effective at visceral pain (Stage 1) than somatic (Stage 2)
All opioids suppress fetal beat-to-beat variability; returns to normal as drug eliminated
5.2 Opioid Agonists
Morphine
Natural opioid; most depressive to neonate of all opioids; may depress uterine contractions → questionable choice for labor
If used at all: only early in labor to maximize time for metabolism before delivery
Meperidine (Demerol)

Meperidine — Critical Neonatal Points
Duration 2–4 h; half-life 3–4.5 h. IM absorption 45–60 min → traditional rule: give only if delivery expected within 1 h OR >3 h away. Active metabolite: normeperidine, t½ = 15–40 hours → neurologic and depressive effects in neonate for DAYS after delivery (fetal liver can produce normeperidine or drug can cross + accumulate). UV:MV 0.81; may exceed 1.0 after 2–3 h. Atropine-like properties: maternal tachycardia + myocardial depression. Unique: enhances cervical effacement/dilation; intrathecal use possible when patient allergic to local anesthetics.
Fentanyl, Sufentanil, Alfentanil, Remifentanil
All: meperidine analogues, lipid-soluble; rapidly metabolized by first-pass through fetal liver to inactive metabolites → depressive effects uncommon with intermittent IV boluses

Remifentanil: esterase-based metabolism, independent of duration + end-organ function; extremely short-acting → only continuous infusion mode. IV-PCA remifentanil: most commonly used non-neuraxial technique (Karol & Weiniger Curr Anesthesiol Rep 2021); inferior to neuraxial but similar satisfaction in 1 single-center RCT (Thorbjonson Eur J Obstet Gynecol 2020); risk: maternal respiratory depression to apnea
Alfentanil IV 10 μg/kg: can cause severe neonatal respiratory depression — not used in standard labor protocols
Fentanyl 1 μg/kg IV just before CS: no neonatal depressive effects detected (Owen Anesth Analg 2002)
Buprenorphine (Opioid Use Disorder Context)

Buprenorphine (Suboxone) ใน Labor — Pain Management Dilemma
μ-receptor affinity: several hundred times greater than any other opioid → effectively blocks μ-receptor → other opioids cannot produce effect. Half-life ≈ 37 hours → takes DAYS to degrade before another opioid can work → traditional labor analgesics AND neuraxial techniques may be fruitless initially. Solution: epidural clonidine (alpha agonist) as substitute for opioid in neuraxial technique (Hoyt et al Int J Obstet Anesth 2018). FDA black box warning on neuraxial clonidine (1997) for fetal bradycardia — but same side-effect profile as traditional epidural; approved in US only for pediatric oncology neuraxial use. Neonatal NAS: buprenorphine-exposed neonates have LOWER initial NAS scores, shorter hospital stay, fewer need treatment vs methadone (Lund et al Subst Abuse 2013).
5.3 Opioid Agonist-Antagonists
κ-receptor agonists → ceiling effect on both analgesia AND respiratory depression — safer re: apnea risk; limited effectiveness

Nalbuphine (Nubain): UV:MV 0.97; actively BLOCKS μ-receptor; ↓ fetal beat-to-beat variability; may produce sinusoidal FHR pattern; can precipitate acute withdrawal in opioid-addicted mother OR fetus — CAUTION with OUD mothers
Butorphanol (Stadol): UV:MV 0.84; essentially no effect on μ-receptor (unlike nalbuphine); inactive metabolites; limited fetal effects
5.4 Opioid Antagonist — Naloxone (Narcan)
Blocks ALL opioid receptors; reverses adverse effects; half-life 30–45 minutes → may need repeat boluses if mother received long-acting opioid
NOT benign: can cause pulmonary edema + cardiac failure → use cautiously
Crosses placenta → reverses fetal opioid effect
NEVER give naloxone to mother addicted to opioids or on OUD maintenance → precipitates acute withdrawal in mother AND addicted fetus/neonate
6. Nitrous Oxide and Obstetric Nerve Blocks
6.1 Nitrous Oxide (N2O)
Mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonist; subanesthetic levels inhaled with O2 → rapid pain relief; highly variable individual response; weak analgesic
US utilization: from few academic centers → 50 centers by 2015 → >500 institutions by 2018; now widely available
Cochrane 2012 (26 studies, 2959 women): flurane derivatives > N2O for pain; N2O > placebo but with N/V, dizziness, drowsiness; N2O = TENS (no difference)
Most nulliparous women or induced labors convert to neuraxial block → bolsters N2O's minimal analgesic potential
Value: gives women a choice; option when neuraxial contraindicated or unavailable; no anesthesia personnel required
Occupational exposure concerns not validated; newer scavenger systems allay fears
Long-term effects on neonate-mother interaction, breastfeeding: NOT adequately measured
6.2 Paracervical Block (PCB)
Purpose: block visceral afferents near dilating cervix; lidocaine WITHOUT epinephrine (preferred); bupivacaine CONTRAINDICATED for PCB — maternal cardiotoxicity
Effective in ~75% of women for Stage 1 analgesia; described in US since 1945

Paracervical Block — Fetal Bradycardia Risk
Post-PCB fetal bradycardia: well documented. Mechanisms: (1) direct injection into fetal head (fetal systemic toxicity); (2) aortocaval compression from positioning; (3) paracervical manipulation; (4) ↑ uterine activity; (5) local anesthetic-induced uterine vasospasm → ↓ perfusion. Fetal deaths reported. Bradycardia usually short + without adverse sequelae but fetal deaths documented. PCB less commonly performed in US today — less satisfaction, more need for subsequent analgesia vs neuraxial (Junttila Int J Obstet Anesth 2009).
6.3 Pudendal Block
Used for late Stage 2 analgesia + operative vaginal delivery with forceps; transvaginal approach; 5–10 mL local anesthetic at sacrospinous ligament–ischial spine junction
Risk: maternal intravascular injection → aspirate frequently
Blood levels of local anesthetic in neonate up to 4 h after delivery; Cochrane 2012: no significant neonatal adverse effects (Apgar <7 at 5 min)
7. Neuraxial Analgesia — Techniques
7.1 Lumbar Epidural

Initiated sitting or lateral position; needle at L4–5, L3–4, or L2–3 interspace; catheter inserted through needle → tested with lidocaine/bupivacaine ± epinephrine to rule out intrathecal/intravascular placement
Maintenance: intermittent bolus ± continuous infusion; trend = dilute local anesthetic + low-dose opioid → minimal drug amounts while maintaining analgesia
Onset: 15–20 minutes; catheter stays for duration of labor → re-dosable for CS if needed
7.2 Patient-Controlled Epidural Analgesia (PCEA)
First described by Gambling et al 1988; allows parturient to self-administer boluses
Up to 35% reduction in total local anesthetic volume (Ranasinghe & Birnbach Int J Women's Health 2009)
Settings: demand bolus, lockout interval, hourly maximum rate; background infusion optional but set at lower rate when included
7.3 Intrathecal Injection (Spinal)
25–27 gauge needle; 'pop' through dura → CSF confirmed → inject → needle removed; no catheter left behind
Maternal blood levels: clinically irrelevant = near-zero fetal exposure — lowest maternal levels of ALL analgesic methods
Modern dosing: preserves temperature, touch, proprioception, even mobility while providing pain relief
Somnolence + respiratory depression reported with small doses of intrathecal opioids (Hays & Palmer Anesthesiology 1994) — maternal, not direct fetal effect
7.4 Combined Spinal-Epidural (CSE)
Onset: 2–5 min (CSE) vs 15–20 min (epidural alone) — 4x faster (Wong Int J Womens Health 2010)
Spinal injection first (rapid onset) → remove spinal needle → thread epidural catheter → epidural catheter used when spinal wears off
Ideal for: rapidly progressing multiparous patient; epidural catheters placed during CSE more likely to function properly
Perceived detriment: epidural catheter untested before spinal wears off → some providers hesitant if emergency arises
7.5 Programmed Intermittent Epidural Bolusing (PIEB)
Principle: intermittent boluses (higher pressure) disperse medication better than continuous infusion → better spread
Benefits: ↓ breakthrough pain; ↓ clinician-assisted supplementation; ↓ total local anesthetic milligrams; ↑ patient satisfaction
Meta-analysis (Wang Medicine 2022): PIEB superior to continuous infusion for analgesia AND maternal/fetal outcomes
8. Maternal Effects of Neuraxial Blocks
8.1 Benefits
Allows mother to relax + conserve energy; prevents hyperventilation → ↑ fetal O2 delivery and acid-base status
↓ catecholamines from pain + stress → ↑ uteroplacental blood flow (Fratelli Br J Anaesth 2011)
Studies: ↑ or no change in uteroplacental blood flow WITH epidural provided hypotension avoided/treated
8.2 Common Side Effects
Hypotension: sympathetic block → vasodilation; more severe after spinal vs epidural; treat with IV fluid bolus ± phenylephrine or ephedrine; ↓ perfusion if untreated → fetal acidosis
Pruritus: more common after spinal; opioid-induced (not histamine-mediated); dose-dependent; usually self-limited; treat with naloxone or nalbuphine

Motor block: some weakness likely with local anesthetic component; most institutions confine parturient to bed; 'walking epidural' shown NOT to alter labor outcome; liability concerns → mostly abandoned
Other opioid side effects: N/V, sedation, urinary retention — all κ/μ-mediated; resolve with time
Respiratory depression: rare but monitor
8.3 Maternal Fever (Epidural-Associated Hyperthermia)

Epidural Fever — Non-Infectious Mechanism → Neonatal Implication
Gradual modest hyperthermia in laboring women with epidural — NOT seen with other analgesia methods or unmedicated labor. More common in nulliparous. Mechanism: NOT infection; likely cytokine-induced inflammatory response (IL-6) — normal labor inflammation. Altered thermoregulation + antipyretic effect of non-epidural opioids partly explain it. Neonatal consequences: poor tone, lower Apgar, assisted ventilation, tracheal intubation, CPR, supplemental O2, early-onset neonatal seizures, ↑ sepsis evaluation + treatment (Arendt & Segal Clin Perinatol 2013). KEY: studies show NO ↑ incidence of ACTUAL neonatal sepsis despite maternal fever. Many NICUs now require OTHER clinical indications besides maternal temperature before initiating sepsis workup.
8.4 Labor Prolongation and Cesarean Section Rate

Epidural + CS rate: association but NOT causation; dysfunctional labor is associated with both more epidural requests AND higher CS rate — labor dystocia = confound, not epidural itself (Segal et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2000)
Stage 1 duration: conflicting data; 2 meta-analyses + Cochrane: NO difference in Stage 1 duration; 2004 meta-analysis: ~30 min longer

Stage 2: statistically significant ~15 min average prolongation; not clinically relevant if fetal status reassuring; ACOG: if epidural in place + no fetal compromise → allow Stage 2 to continue until fetal descent to lower station
Prolonged Stage 2 allowed → vaginal delivery more likely; section rate reduced ~50% vs adhering to strict guidelines (Gimovsky Berghella Am J Obstet Gynecol 2016)
8.5 Inadvertent Injections and High Blocks
Intravascular injection (catheter in vessel): large local anesthetic dose → maternal seizure/arrest → fetal effect from maternal hypoxia (not primarily from drug level)
Intrathecal injection (catheter through dura): respiratory muscle paralysis → apnea if unrecognized → maternal arrest → obvious fetal consequences
Both exceedingly rare and very preventable with good technique; test doses + aliquoted dosing crucial
9. Fetal and Neonatal Effects of Labor Analgesia
9.1 Acid-Base Balance
Key benefit of neuraxial: prevents maternal hyperventilation → prevents maternal hypocapnia → maintains normal oxyhemoglobin curve → ↑ O2 to fetus → ↑ fetal acid-base status
Literature: fetal acid-base status BETTER with epidural than systemic opioids OR no analgesia (Reynolds Br J Obstet Gynecol 2002; meta-analysis)
Neonatal base excess at delivery: stays within normal limits more consistently with epidural vs other techniques; less meconium; comparable or improved Apgar scores
9.2 Fetal Heart Rate Changes
Fetal bradycardia: 10–12% of parturients receiving any neuraxial analgesia; onset 15–45 min after initiation
Mechanism: ↓ maternal plasma epinephrine (rapid) vs less rapid ↓ norepinephrine → temporary tocolytic/tocodynamic imbalance → uterine hypertonus → ↓ perfusion → bradycardia
Management: conservative therapy + intrauterine resuscitative measures; rarely leads to emergency delivery; Apgar scores typically fine
FHR changes: more common with systemic IV opioids vs neuraxial; more newborns of IV opioid mothers: ↓ 1-min Apgar + needed naloxone/resuscitation vs neuraxial
Loss of beat-to-beat variability: large doses neuraxial opioids (also seen with systemic opioids) — temporally limited
9.3 Breastfeeding
Common claim: epidurals damage neonate's breastfeeding ability — NOT supported by peer-reviewed literature
Reynolds review (17 studies 1994–2010): only 2 studies showed dose-related detrimental effect of epidural fentanyl — NOT confirmed by other retrospective studies
Canadian study 2010: breastfeeding success >95% achievable after epidural bupivacaine + fentanyl with no dose-related effect (with good support)

Where policy required different postpartum management for epidural patients (minimized early maternal-infant contact) → breastfeeding impacted; where early contact maintained → no effect of epidural on lactation
10. Anesthesia for Operative and Cesarean Delivery
10.1 Operative Vaginal Delivery
Vacuum extraction: well tolerated by mother; minimal additional analgesia needed; local perineum anesthetic for episiotomy sufficient
Forceps: placement very stimulating; neuraxial preferred; pudendal block acceptable (faster placement than neuraxial)
If forceps attempt fails → epidural or CSE can be extended to higher level for CS — advantage of having catheter in place
10.2 Neuraxial Anesthesia for Cesarean Section
Neuraxial = preferred for elective or urgent CS; avoid maternal airway management + aspiration risk
If epidural already in place: switch to concentrated, faster-onset local anesthetic → test doses + aliquoted dosing still needed (catheter may shift when patient moved)
All neuraxial techniques: better neonatal Apgar, less assisted ventilation, fewer NICU admissions vs general anesthesia (Afolabi Cochrane 2012; Beckmann ANZJOG 2012)
Failed maternal intubation also associated with NICU admissions
10.3 General Anesthesia for Cesarean Section

Failed Intubation in Pregnant Women — Critical Risk
Failed intubation incidence: 1:808 general anesthetics for CS (Reale et al Anesthesiology 2022; multicenter n>14,000); difficult intubation: 1:49. 8–10x higher than non-pregnant population. Reasons: edematous airways (fluid accumulation from hormonal changes), Valsalva effect of Stage 2, obesity, altered GI motility. Rescue equipment: supraglottic airway devices + video laryngoscopes ↓ fatalities. Aspiration risk: ↑ gastric volume, delayed emptying, ↓ lower esophageal tone in pregnancy. Pre-induction aspiration prophylaxis: sodium citrate (oral antacid), ± famotidine, ± metoclopramide — none have fetal impact.
Induction Agents (UV:MV reference: Table in Section 3)
Propofol: most commonly used in US; UV:MV 0.7; short half-life; minimal residual impact; depresses maternal myocardium but returns to baseline if hypotension treated
Ketamine: UV:MV 0.54; reserved for hemorrhaging/volume-depleted patients — less cardio-depressive + ↑ sympathetic response; maintains hemodynamics
Etomidate: UV:MV 0.5; reserved for cardiac disease patients
All induction agents cross placenta BUT: (1) fetal liver first-pass metabolism; (2) dilutional effect in fetal circulation → rarely direct neonatal effect at standard doses
If abruption (fetoplacental compromise): little transfer because compromised perfusion
Neuromuscular Blockade
Succinylcholine: agent of choice for rapid sequence intubation; fastest onset; t½ ≈ 90 sec (pseudocholinesterase); metabolized to benign metabolites → essentially no fetal exposure

Nondepolarizing agents (vecuronium, pancuronium): hydrophilic + highly ionized → UV:MV 0.11–0.19 → very little crosses placenta; use when succinylcholine contraindicated or prolonged intubation needed
Volatile Agents + N2O

Isoflurane/sevoflurane: pass to fetus; rarely direct effect if induction-to-delivery <15 min; if >15 min → concentrations equilibrate → neonatal depression (usually ventilatory support for few minutes as agents expire through lungs)
Uterine incision-to-delivery >180 sec → neonate more likely depressed from COMPROMISED PERFUSION, not volatile agents (Datta et al Obstet Gynecol 1981)
No data showing fetal neurotoxicity or neurodevelopmental delays from maternal CS under general anesthesia (Sprung Anesthesiology 2009; Robbins Int J Obstet Anesth 2021)
11. Tranexamic Acid (TXA) in Obstetric Hemorrhage

TXA สำหรับ Postpartum Hemorrhage — Neonatal Safety
Mechanism: antifibrinolytic. WOMAN Trial (Lancet 2017): IV TXA → 19% ↓ relative risk of death from bleeding in PPH (mortality 1.5% TXA vs 1.9% placebo; RR=0.81, 95% CI 0.65–1.0; P=.045). FDA Category B (no evidence of risk in humans). Neonatal exposure: guidelines recommend use ONLY after umbilical cord clamping; lactating mothers express <1% of TXA into breast milk following IV treatment; no adverse outcomes reported in breastfed infants. Safety data mostly from neonates undergoing cardiac surgery — reassuring. TXA prophylaxis for CS: currently under investigation (Sentilhes NEJM 2021).
12. NICU Practical — รับทารกหลัง Labor Analgesia / Anesthesia
12.1 หลัง Systemic Opioids
Meperidine: watch for respiratory depression + sedation ≥24–48 h; normeperidine t½ 15–40 h → effects นาน; naloxone PRN: คำนวณ dose แต่ระวัง t½ naloxone สั้นกว่า normeperidine — อาจต้องซ้ำ
IV-PCA remifentanil/fentanyl: monitor O2 saturation + sedation; oxygen supplementation + observation usually sufficient
Agonist-antagonists (nalbuphine): sinusoidal FHR pattern possible; watch for sedation; if OUD mother → neonatal withdrawal monitoring
Naloxone to OUD mother: DO NOT give — precipitates acute withdrawal in neonate; manage neonatal respiratory depression with supportive care
12.2 หลัง Epidural/Neuraxial
Expect BETTER acid-base status vs no-analgesia or systemic opioids — reassuring baseline
Epidural fever (non-infectious): do NOT automatically initiate full sepsis workup based solely on maternal temperature; require additional clinical indications per NICU policy
Fetal bradycardia 15–45 min post-epidural: usually resolved before delivery; Apgar typically normal; if born during bradycardia → resuscitation per NRP 2020
High block/inadvertent IV injection → maternal hypoxia → fetal acidosis → resuscitation readiness
12.3 หลัง General Anesthesia (CS)
Short induction-to-delivery time + short-acting agents = minimal neonatal effect
If delivery delayed >15 min after induction: volatile agent equilibrated → neonatal depression; support ventilation until agents expire
Induction-to-delivery >180 sec: depression likely from compromised perfusion, not drugs; assess for hypoxia-ischemia → HIE protocol if indicated
Failed intubation: maternal hypoxia → neonatal hypoxia; be ready for aggressive resuscitation
Diazepam for eclamptic seizures: expect neonate with hypotonia, hypothermia, respiratory depression lasting up to 8 days; supportive care + thermal support
13. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Meperidine: Normeperidine นาน 8 วัน
Meperidine UV:MV 0.81 แต่ active metabolite normeperidine มี t½ = 15–40 h → neurologic + depressive effects ใน neonate หลายวันหลังคลอด. ดังนั้น: ถ้า birth history ระบุว่าแม่ได้ meperidine ช่วงคลอด → watch respiratory effort, tone, feeding activity ≥24–48 h; naloxone ช่วยได้แต่ต้องซ้ำ (t½ naloxone 30–45 min < normeperidine). Meperidine ไม่ควรเป็น first-line อีกต่อไปสำหรับ labor analgesia แต่ยังคงใช้อยู่บางที่.

PEARL 2 — Epidural Fever ≠ Chorioamnionitis ≠ Neonatal Sepsis
Epidural hyperthermia เกิดจาก cytokine-induced inflammation (IL-6) ไม่ใช่ infection. ไม่มีหลักฐานว่า neonatal sepsis เพิ่มขึ้น แม้แม่จะมี fever จาก epidural. หลาย NICU ปรับ policy: ต้องมี clinical sign อื่นนอกจาก maternal fever ก่อน initiate sepsis workup. รู้จัก epidural fever → ลด unnecessary neonatal intervention (blood culture, antibiotics, NICU stay) ซึ่งมี harm ของตัวเอง.

PEARL 3 — Spinal = Nearly Zero Fetal Drug Exposure
Route of administration: IV > paracervical > IM > epidural > spinal (lowest). Spinal drug levels in maternal blood: clinically irrelevant. ดังนั้น: neonatal depression หลัง spinal = look for OTHER causes (hypoxia from maternal hypotension, prolonged delivery, etc.) ไม่ใช่ direct drug effect. Epidural: low levels; high-dose or rapidly dosed epidural = more maternal absorption + potential fetal effect.

PEARL 4 — Buprenorphine (OUD) → Labor Pain Dilemma + NAS Better than Methadone
μ-receptor affinity buprenorphine > other opioids หลายร้อยเท่า + t½ 37 h → traditional opioids/epidural บางครั้ง fruitless ทันที. Solution: epidural clonidine ใน OUD patients (Hoyt et al 2018). แต่ข่าวดีสำหรับ NICU: buprenorphine-exposed neonates มี lower NAS scores, shorter hospital stay, fewer needing treatment vs methadone-exposed (Lund Subst Abuse 2013). ดังนั้น: buprenorphine = better choice for mother on OUD → neonatal NAS management easier.

PEARL 5 — General Anesthesia: 15-Minute and 180-Second Rules
Rule 1: induction-to-delivery >15 min → volatile agents equilibrate → neonatal depression (เพราะ equilibrate กับ fetal circulation). Rule 2: uterine incision-to-delivery >180 sec → depression จาก compromised perfusion ไม่ใช่ drugs (Datta 1981). ดังนั้น: brief bag-mask ventilation ช่วยทารกที่ depress จาก volatile = usually sufficient; ถ้า prolonged induction-to-delivery + uterine incision-to-delivery = high risk for hypoxia-ischemia → assess for HIE criteria.
14. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Melzack R et al Can Med Assoc J 1981;125:357: labor pain more intense than almost any other known pain syndrome — basis for aggressive pain management in modern obstetrics
Reynolds F et al Br J Obstet Gynecol 2002;109:1344 (Meta-analysis): epidural analgesia: ↑ fetal acid-base status vs systemic opioids and vs no analgesia; ↓ fetal acidosis in 1st and 2nd stages
Anim-Somuah M et al Cochrane 2011;12:CD000331: every study comparing neuraxial vs other analgesic technique: neuraxial superior for pain relief
Madden K et al Cochrane 2016;5:CD009356: hypnosis: may ↓ analgesic use but not epidural use; no clear differences in spontaneous vaginal birth or satisfaction
Klomp T et al Cochrane 2012;9:CD009351: inhaled analgesia (N2O): flurane derivatives > N2O; N2O > placebo but more side effects; N2O = TENS (no difference)
Stocki D et al Anesth Analg 2014;118:589 (RCT): remifentanil IV-PCA vs epidural: epidural superior for labor pain; remifentanil risk maternal respiratory depression/apnea
Gambling GR et al 1988: first description of PCEA for labor — up to 35% reduction in total local anesthetic volume
Wong CA et al Int J Womens Health 2010;1:139: CSE onset 2–5 min vs epidural 15–20 min (4x faster); review of neuraxial technique advances
Wang XX et al Medicine 2022;101:e28742 (Meta-analysis RCTs): PIEB superior to continuous epidural infusion for analgesia AND maternal/fetal outcomes
Segal S et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2000;183:974: rapid change in epidural availability → NOT ↑ CS rate → epidural not causally linked to cesarean delivery
Arendt KW & Segal BS Clin Perinatol 2013;40:385: epidural fever: cytokine-induced inflammation (IL-6); not infectious; no ↑ neonatal sepsis despite maternal temperature
Gimovsky AC & Berghella V Am J Obstet Gynecol 2016;214:361 (RCT): extended 2nd stage with epidural: ↓ CS rate ~50% vs standard guidelines; vaginal delivery more likely
Reale SC et al Anesthesiology 2022;136:697 (n>14,000): difficult intubation CS: 1:49; failed intubation: 1:808; reaffirms obstetric airway as high-risk
Afolabi BB & Lesi FE Cochrane 2012: regional vs general anesthesia for CS: regional → better Apgar, less assisted ventilation, fewer NICU admissions
Datta S et al Obstet Gynecol 1981;58:331: uterine incision-to-delivery >180 sec: neonate depressed primarily from compromised perfusion not volatile agents
Sprung J et al Anesthesiology 2009;111:302: CS under general anesthesia: NO association with learning disabilities or neurodevelopmental delay in population-based birth cohort
Lund IO et al Subst Abuse 2013;7:61: buprenorphine vs methadone for OUD in pregnancy: buprenorphine-exposed neonates = lower NAS scores, shorter hospital, fewer needing treatment
Hoyt MR et al Int J Obstet Anesth 2018;34:67: epidural clonidine for labor analgesia in buprenorphine OUD patients: efficacious; no fetal bradycardia at doses used
WOMAN Trial Collaborators Lancet 2017;389:2105: TXA for PPH: 19% ↓ relative risk of death from bleeding (RR=0.81, 95% CI 0.65–1.0, P=.045); now recommended for all PPH
Sentilhes L et al NEJM 2021;384:1623: prophylactic TXA at CS: ongoing investigation; Lancet RCT for PPH prevention
Eisenach JC et al Pain 2008;140:87: severity of acute pain after childbirth (independent of delivery mode) predicts persistent pain and postpartum depression — supports aggressive pain management
15. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ยาอะไรที่ให้แม่ระหว่างคลอดจะมีโอกาสผ่าน blood-brain barrier ของแม่เสมอ?

ตอบ: ยาทุกชนิดที่ผ่าน maternal blood-brain barrier มีคุณสมบัติผ่าน placenta ด้วย เพราะ transport mechanism เหมือนกัน: nonionized + lipophilic + unbound fraction ผ่าน membrane. ถ้ายา produce cerebral effect ในแม่ → likely crosses to fetus. Degree ขึ้นกับ pKa, lipophilicity, protein binding, UV:MV ratio, และ timing relative to delivery.
Q: Fetal ion trapping คืออะไร และเกิดในสถานการณ์ใด?

ตอบ: Fetal pH < maternal pH (fetal acidosis) → drug ที่ข้ามมาในฝั่ง fetal circulation convert จาก nonionized → ionized form มากขึ้น (weak base + lower pH → more ionized) → ไม่สามารถกลับข้ามไปฝั่งแม่ได้ → สะสมใน fetal circulation. เกิดบ่อยใน: fetal distress, prolonged labor, local anesthetic toxicity scenarios. Meperidine UV:MV อาจเกิน 1.0 หลัง 2–3 h เพราะ ion trapping + accumulation.
Q: เหตุใด IV-PCA remifentanil จึงเป็นที่นิยมมากขึ้นสำหรับ non-neuraxial labor analgesia?

ตอบ: Remifentanil: esterase-based metabolism, independent of duration and end-organ function → extremely short-acting (continuous infusion mode) → pain relief between contractions without prolonged accumulation. เป็น most commonly used non-neuraxial technique (Karol & Weiniger 2021). แต่: inferior to neuraxial for pain; maternal respiratory depression to apnea = real risk → ต้องมี continuous monitoring; mild neonatal depression possible.
Q: Nalbuphine แตกต่างจาก butorphanol อย่างไรในแง่ opioid receptor pharmacology และ clinical risk?

ตอบ: Nalbuphine: κ-agonist + ACTIVE μ-blocker; butorphanol: κ-agonist + essentially NO μ effect. Nalbuphine ที่ขนาด labor analgesia สามารถ precipitate acute opioid withdrawal ในแม่ที่ OUD หรือ maintenance program และใน addicted neonate. Nalbuphine ยังลด fetal beat-to-beat variability และอาจทำให้ sinusoidal FHR pattern. Butorphanol ปลอดภัยกว่าในแง่ μ-receptor — ไม่เกิด withdrawal.
Q: เหตุใด epidural fever จึงไม่ควร trigger neonatal sepsis workup โดยอัตโนมัติ?

ตอบ: Epidural fever = non-infectious cytokine-induced inflammation (IL-6), ไม่ใช่ chorioamnionitis. Studies show NO ↑ in actual neonatal sepsis incidence despite maternal fever from epidural (Curtin 2015; Sultan 2016). การทำ full sepsis workup โดยไม่มี clinical indications เพิ่มเติม = unjustified intervention + harm (pain, antibiotic exposure, maternal-infant separation). Best practice: require additional clinical signs (leukocytosis, maternal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, fetal distress, neonatal signs) ก่อน workup.
Q: Buprenorphine-maintained OUD patient มาคลอด: ทำไม traditional labor analgesics ไม่ทำงาน และ solution คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Buprenorphine affinity ต่อ μ-receptor > other opioids หลายร้อยเท่า → blocks μ-receptor effectively → traditional opioids cannot displace → no analgesia. t½ = 37 h → takes days to degrade → epidural opioid fruitless ระยะแรก. Solution: epidural clonidine (α2 agonist) แทน opioid ใน epidural mixture (Hoyt 2018): allows patient to maintain buprenorphine dose, avoid opioid exposure, receive reliable labor analgesia.
Q: ถ้า CS ใช้ general anesthesia และ induction-to-delivery ใช้เวลา 20 นาที: คาดว่าทารกจะมีปัญหาอะไร?

ตอบ: Volatile agents (isoflurane/sevoflurane): equilibrate กับ fetal circulation ภายใน 15 min → ทารกจะ depress → ต้องการ assisted ventilation. Good news: volatile agents expire through neonatal lungs → support ventilation สักครู่ → ทารกฟื้นเอง. แยกจาก: ถ้า uterine incision-to-delivery >180 sec ด้วย → depression ส่วนหนึ่งจาก compromised perfusion → ประเมิน HIE criteria. ไม่มีหลักฐานว่า single GA exposure ทำให้ neonatal neurodevelopmental delay ในระยะยาว (Sprung 2009).
Q: TXA ใน postpartum hemorrhage: ควร give ก่อนหรือหลัง cord clamping? เหตุผลคืออะไร?

ตอบ: Guidelines: give TXA AFTER umbilical cord clamping. เหตุผล: (1) ลด neonatal exposure; (2) lactating mothers express <1% of TXA into breast milk; no adverse outcomes in breastfed infants. FDA Category B — no evidence of risk in humans. Safety data: mostly from cardiac surgery neonates — reassuring. WOMAN Trial 2017: TXA ↓ mortality from PPH bleeding 19% (RR=0.81, P=.045) — life-saving intervention; do not withhold if indicated.

Day 124 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.27
บทต่อไป: Chapter 27 — Physical Examination of the Healthy Newborn. เตรียมตัว: systematic newborn physical exam; normal vs abnormal findings ตาม gestational age; skin findings (erythema toxicum, milia, mongolian spots, hemangiomas); cranial anatomy (sutures, fontanelles, caput vs cephalhematoma vs subgaleal); cardiac auscultation in newborn; hip dysplasia screening (Ortolani/Barlow); newborn reflexes + tone assessment; genitalia + anal examination; birth marks significance.
